name: Marid
description: The Marid (Arabic: مارد, plural: ماردة māridah) are powerful and arrogant jinn in Arabian and Islamic folklore. They are often described as the most potent type of jinn, possessing immense strength and magical abilities. Unlike some other jinn, Marid are not easily controlled or bound, and they require significant skill and power to command. They are associated with water, particularly oceans and seas, and are often depicted as dwelling in underwater palaces or hidden grottos. Marid are known for their pride and their tendency to grant wishes in a twisted or ironic manner, often leading to unintended consequences. They are intelligent and cunning, and are not easily fooled. In some tales, they are depicted as benevolent beings who can offer great assistance to those who treat them with respect, while in others, they are portrayed as malevolent entities who delight in causing chaos and destruction. Their immense power makes them formidable adversaries, and their capricious nature makes them unpredictable allies.
subtitle: Powerful and arrogant jinn of Arabian folklore
appearance: Marid are often described as being immense in size and possessing a fearsome appearance. They can take many forms, but are often depicted as towering figures with fiery eyes and smoke emanating from their bodies. They can also appear as monstrous sea creatures or as handsome humans, depending on their intentions.
habitat: Oceans, seas, underwater palaces, and hidden grottos.
behavior: Marid are known for their pride, arrogance, and capriciousness. They are intelligent and cunning, and are not easily controlled. They may grant wishes, but often in a twisted or ironic manner. Their behavior can range from benevolent to malevolent, depending on their mood and the respect they are shown.

keyMyths.title: The Fisherman and the Jinni
keyMyths.description: This classic tale from One Thousand and One Nights features a fisherman who accidentally releases a Marid from a bottle. The Marid, initially grateful, quickly turns vengeful and threatens to kill the fisherman, only to be outsmarted and imprisoned again through trickery.

keyMyths.title: The Tale of Prince Ahmed and the Fairy Pari Banou
keyMyths.description: In this story, Prince Ahmed encounters the Fairy Pari Banou, who is served by numerous Marid. These Marid demonstrate their immense power by transporting the Prince and creating magnificent palaces at Pari Banou's command. They show both servitude and power, reflecting the complex nature of Marid.

keyMyths.title: Aladdin and the Genie of the Lamp
keyMyths.description: While the genie in Aladdin is often portrayed as a less powerful jinn, some interpretations suggest he could be a Marid due to his immense power and ability to grant wishes. The story explores the dangers and rewards of controlling such a being, and the potential for wishes to backfire.

sources.author: Mia Bloom
sources.work: Dying to Kill: The Allure of Suicide Terror
sources.description: Provides context on the historical and cultural significance of Jinn in Islamic thought, indirectly shedding light on the Marid's place within that framework.
sources.text: Provides context on the historical and cultural significance of Jinn in Islamic thought, indirectly shedding light on the Marid's place within that framework.

sources.author: Robert Irwin
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to the One Thousand and One Nights, detailing the various types of jinn and their roles in the stories, including the Marid.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to the One Thousand and One Nights, detailing the various types of jinn and their roles in the stories, including the Marid.

sources.author: Theresa Bane
sources.work: Encyclopedia of Demons in World Religions and Cultures
sources.description: Offers a detailed overview of demonic entities across various cultures, including the Marid, exploring their origins, characteristics, and significance.
